note: Remote cold model-load ~2min per swap vs ~14s warm, observed RTX PRO 4000 Pod (MPI-324 sweep), consistent across Krea2/Chroma so per-model cold cost not model-specific. Users swap models often -> bad UX. Hypotheses: network-volume read into RAM/VRAM, small-VRAM offload, cold GPU warmup. User caveat: RTX PRO 4000 may just be a weak inference card - retest on 4090/5090 before treating as a real bug. Not a 1.1 blocker.

Moved MPI-329 from To do to To do.

Cold-load breakdown captured live 2026-07-23 on a 4090 dev Pod (v0.17.0-dev-cu130, ComfyUI 0.28), LTX 2.3 High i2v_ms, total 159.93s. Sampling is NOT the cost: steps run at 2.54 s/it. The time is load/prep - roughly 50s of text-encoder load alone (17:11:51 to 17:12:41), a 39.72s first step that is dynamic-VRAM load amortised into stage 1, and a SECOND full 40050MB 'prepared for dynamic VRAM loading' at the stage boundary (the model is re-prepared per stage, not once per gen). Crucially the 42GB was already hot-store staged to CONTAINER DISK, so none of this is network-volume I/O - optimising volume reads would move none of it. Caveat: LTX High is bf16 on a 24GB card = the MPI-197 worst case, so run the Balanced tier on the same Pod to separate cold-load cost from bf16 thrash before sizing a fix. Detail in research/2026-07-23-ltx-cold-load-breakdown.md.

WARM datapoint, same Pod/session/settings (LTX 2.3 High i2v_ms, 3s 512x960, RTX 4090, ComfyUI 0.28): 1m27s warm vs 2m40s cold, so warmth saves ~73s. Still 87s for a clip whose steady-state sampling is 2.54 s/it, i.e. warm runs remain load/prep-dominated too - caching whatever the cold run pays for does NOT get this near sampling cost. Both runs used a model already hot-store staged to container disk, so neither figure includes network-volume I/O.

KREA2 COLD-vs-WARM, the sharpest datapoint yet (2026-07-23, same session, same model, same op). Pod RTX 4090 on v0.17.0-dev-cu130 / ComfyUI 0.28: cold 1m02s, consecutive warm 8s -> cold-load penalty 54s. LOCAL RTX 4060 Ti on the 0.28 portable: cold 37s, warm 23s -> penalty 14s. So WARM the Pod is ~3x the local machine (correct for 4090 vs 4060 Ti), but COLD it is 1.7x SLOWER than the weaker local box, because it pays ~4x the cold-load penalty. Same model, same operation, both cold - the delta is the Pod load path alone, not model size, not the GPU, and not network-volume I/O (weights were hot-store staged to container disk). Pair this with the LTX breakdown in research/: there, ~50s of a 63s pre-sampling phase was text-encoder load and the model was re-prepared for dynamic VRAM loading once PER STAGE.

CORRECTION to the previous Krea2 entry, caught by the user (disk stayed at 42GB/84% = LTX only, so Krea2 never staged). Krea2 was NOT hot-store staged and was therefore reading from the NETWORK VOLUME, not container disk. Cause found and it is not disk space or eviction: HOT_STORE_MIN_GB = 20 in js/services/commandExecutor.js filters the stage list to weights >= 20GB, and Krea2's largest file is krea2-raw-transformer at 13.49GB - so Krea2 is never even REQUESTED for staging. Consequences: (1) the LTX figures stand as non-volume-I/O (42GB was staged to disk); (2) the Krea2 figures DO include volume reads, so the Pod-vs-local Krea2 comparison is not apples-to-apples - local reads a local SSD while the Pod pulls ~13.5GB over a network volume, which is a strong candidate for the 54s vs 14s cold-penalty gap. ACTIONABLE: the 20GB threshold means our most-used image model gets zero hot-store benefit, ever. But lowering it is not free - CONTAINER_DISK_GB is 50 and LTX High alone occupies 42GB, so staging Krea2 would evict LTX and thrash on every model switch. The threshold and the container disk size have to be sized together.

DECISIVE datapoint 2026-07-23: hot-store staging is NOT the lever, and the earlier 'lower HOT_STORE_MIN_GB' idea is largely DISPROVEN before being built. Third gen of the session, LTX 2.3 High i2v: the 42GB transformer is STILL hot-store staged on container disk (Pod disk held at 42GB/84% the whole session, never evicted - Krea2 is under the 20GB threshold so it never staged and never displaced it). Krea2 running in between evicted LTX from VRAM but not from disk. Result: the app sat at 'LOADING MODEL 1/3 100% 1:57' - roughly two minutes to start generating with ZERO network-volume reads, on a warm Pod (20+ min uptime, ComfyUI already up, third gen). So the cost is not volume I/O, not the download path and not cold boot: it is weight load from disk into VRAM plus the dynamic-VRAM prep, and it costs ~2 minutes whether the source is a network volume or local NVMe. Corollary: staging Krea2 by lowering the threshold would buy far less than the Krea2 cold-vs-warm gap suggested. Attack VRAM load and the per-stage 'prepared for dynamic VRAM loading' re-prep instead.

Run 3 completed: i2v_ms_003, 3m07s total - but at 1216x704, NOT the 512x960 of runs 1-2, so do NOT read it as a regression against the 2m40s cold run. 1216x704 is ~1.74x the pixels. Load was observed at ~1:57 ('LOADING MODEL 1/3 100% 1:57'), leaving roughly 70s of sampling for the larger frame, which tracks. Session table: run1 512x960 fully cold 2m40s; run2 512x960 warm 1m27s; run3 1216x704 VRAM-evicted-but-disk-staged 3m07s. The durable finding is unchanged - ~2 minutes of load on a model that was already resident on container disk.

WORST-CASE datapoint and the ROOT PATTERN, 2026-07-23. Head Swap (Qwen-Edit) on the 4090 dev Pod: got prompt 17:33:41, inference started 17:35:56, ended 17:36:01 - 'Prompt executed in 140.12 seconds'. That is 135s of LOAD and ~5s of inference: 96% overhead. Step trace shows it plainly: 1/4 at 97.32s/it (load amortised into step 1) then 1.28 / 1.56 / 1.42 s/it, so real sampling is about six seconds. This is a ~13GB-class model, not the 42GB LTX. THE PATTERN across the session: back-to-back on the SAME model is fast (2nd Krea2 gen = 8s total), but every model SWITCH costs ~2 minutes (LTX after Krea2 = ~117s load; Qwen after LTX = ~135s load). So this is a model-SWITCHING cost, not cold start and not volume I/O - staging to local disk changed nothing. 13GB at even 1GB/s would be ~13s, so 135s is far beyond byte movement; suspect the aimdo dynamic-VRAM path ('prepared for dynamic VRAM loading', 'patches attached', 'Force pre-loaded N weights'), not I/O. PRODUCT CONSEQUENCE (user's words: 'this is making RunPod useless'): the Pod amortises fine on long video jobs but is near-useless for quick image edits, which is most app usage - a user alternating two models pays the toll on EVERY gen. Also seen: 'Model WanVAE prepared for dynamic VRAM loading. 241MB Staged.' printed THREE times in two seconds for one tiny model, which suggests prepare may run per reference rather than per model - worth checking as a contributor.

CLARIFICATION to the previous entry (user): the Head Swap run used the HYPER speed tier (~13% of time), so the ~5s / 4-step inference is a Hyper figure, not Qwen-Edit's baseline. The '96% load' ratio is therefore tier-specific - on Quality the sampling would be several times longer and the split would read closer to 75/25. What does NOT change is the absolute number: 135s of load, identical whatever tier is chosen. That sharpens rather than softens the product point - the faster the tier the user picks, the more completely load dominates, so Hyper buys almost nothing on a Pod because the two minutes in front of it do not move. Head Swap itself PASSED: appHeadSwap_001, 896x1152, 2m20s total (matches 'Prompt executed in 140.12 seconds'), both heads landed on the correct bodies, so the app's box -> injectionParams -> headSwapInjector wiring survived the ComfyUI 0.28 core bump.
